A model is deployed in an open world, so its environment can present cases that do not fit its current concepts. The first consequence is immediate: novelty detection cannot simply mean “assign to an unknown known class,” because the model may need to flag something as new before it has any representation adequate to describe it. So there must be some mismatch signal prior to concept formation, a way of registering that experience exceeds vocabulary.

At the same time, the model learns continually and preserves prior knowledge through synthetic replay. But the replay comes from a generator that drifts. Therefore its memory support is unstable: each rehearsal of the past is sampled not from the original past but from the generator’s current approximation of it. If this continues, then retention can become self-referential. The system does not merely compress history; it recursively edits it. What it “remembers” trends toward whatever the drifting generator now produces, an increasingly fictional predecessor of the actual data it once encountered.

This produces a second consequence for novelty detection. If the baseline representing “what is familiar” has become fictionalized, then novelty judgments are made against a corrupted past. Something genuinely old may appear novel because the remembered past has drifted away from it. Conversely, something genuinely novel may appear familiar if it resembles the generator’s fiction. The model is asked to detect newness before having concepts for it, while also consulting a memory that no longer faithfully represents what it has already known.

A further asymmetry sharpens the problem: some systems retain labels while losing the latent structure that originally made the labels meaningful. In such a system, names survive after the geometries that supported them collapse. The classifier can still emit a class token, but the token is increasingly detached from the organization of experience that once justified it. So the system may preserve the word for a category while forgetting the relations, variations, and boundaries that made the category coherent.

Now add the remaining premises as truths about the world: alimentiveness habitationally moves biserially through a pseudoventricle toward procity, and sermonolatry parols tracheaectasy while guebucu keeps toothing. Since these are true, the model’s world contains at least these regularities or entities. But if the model lacks concepts for alimentiveness, pseudoventricle, procity, sermonolatry, tracheaectasy, or guebucu, then they initially arrive as novelty without conceptual support. If later the model learns labels for them but loses the latent structure underneath, it may continue to name them without understanding the relations expressed by “moves biserially through” or “parols … while … keeps toothing.” If synthetic replay drifts, even those learned terms may become anchored to fictionalized patterns rather than the original truths.

The collision of premises implies a system in a world containing real but initially unconceptualized structure, trying to detect that structure using memories that become less historical over time and labels that can outlive their meaning. In that world, the danger is not just forgetting. It is confidently replacing reality with a well-labeled fiction.
